1:
The chapter opens on the field of Gilboa with what Matthew Henry reads as a day of solemn reckoning. Now, he says, the day of recompense has come, in which Saul must account for the blood of the Amalekites he had sinfully spared, that of the priests of Nob he had more sinfully spilt, and even the blood of David he had thirsted to shed — the very fall David had foreseen when he said Saul would one day descend into battle and perish (ch. 26). The Israelites make no stand; they flee and fall, and Henry marks the grim providence that those who had followed Saul in his sin went before him in his fall, the troops once used to hunt David now cut down in disorder.

The grammatical-historical readers set the scene rather than moralize it. Adam Clarke takes the verse as the direct continuation of the narrative broken off at chapter 29, the Israelites thrown into confusion at the first onset and turning their backs at once. Charles Ellicott hears in the abrupt opening the reluctance of the narrator himself — a devoted patriot, for whom it was bitter to write the story of that fatal day, yet who knew the punishment of the rejected king had to be recorded. He picks up the dropped threads of the march into Jezreel and the terror that had driven Saul to En-dor, and hurries curtly over an unwelcome task. Keil and Delitzsch frame the whole chapter as of a piece with Saul's life since the day of his rejection: without repentance or remorse he ends by his own hand, only to be abused in death and at last honourably buried by grateful men — a movement mirrored, they note, in the parallel record of 1 Chron. 10.

Beneath the differing emphases the witnesses converge on one reading of the disaster: it is judgment, but not blind fate. Henry draws the sober lesson that we cannot judge of the spiritual or eternal state of any by the manner of their death, since all things come alike to all — the same event befalls the righteous and the wicked in battle. The rout that scatters Israel is at once the harvest of Saul's long defiance and the dark doorway through which, as Henry and the others will insist across the chapter, God is quietly clearing the way for David and for his own glory.
2:
The sword that spares no one falls first on Saul's sons, and it is Jonathan whose name the witnesses cannot pass over. Matthew Henry grieves to find him here — that wise, valiant, good man, who was as much David's friend as Saul was his enemy — falling with the rest though he never shared his father's guilt. Duty would not let him stay home, and Providence so orders it that he falls in the common fate of his family. Here, Henry says, is the one exception that breaks the maxim of Eliphaz, Who ever perished being innocent? (Job 4:7) — for here was one.

Why should the innocent son die? The witnesses answer with one voice that God was clearing David's road to the throne. Henry offers three reasons: to complete the judgment on Saul's house, to make David's way to the crown more clear and open, and to secure that God was to have all the glory, lest it be said that Jonathan had made him king. John Wesley presses the same providence exactly: Jonathan, of all Saul's sons the fairest for the crown, is taken away for the farther exercise of David's faith and patience, that David might depend upon God alone for his crown, and to prevent the divisions over the succession that Jonathan's popularity would surely have stirred. Both note the mercy folded into the affliction — the loss makes David mindful of his own frailty even as it eases his accession. Wesley adds the record's own note, that Abinadab is the Ishui named earlier (1 Sam. 14:49), and that Ishbosheth was left at home, unfit for action, and so survived to be set up later at Mahanaim.

Charles Ellicott, quoting Stanley, paints the scene in broken snatches, as in the short scene of a battle acted upon the stage — the king's figure emerging from the darkness amid showers of Philistine arrows, his three sons fallen before him. Yet Ellicott turns at once to the sequel: the historian, so stern in repressing feeling here, will in the next book copy out David's stately elegy from the Book of Jashar (2 Sam. 1:23), where Saul and Jonathan are lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death not divided. So the witnesses hold together the horror of Jonathan's fall and the tenderness of his memory, refusing to let the good son be lost in the ruin of the bad king.
3:
With his sons dead around him the archers close on Saul himself, and here the witnesses divide over a single Hebrew word. The older reading, kept by Adam Clarke, is that Saul was sore wounded of the archers — grievously hit, beyond hope of recovery, which alone explains why he would beg to be dispatched rather than fight on or flee. Clarke follows Houbigant, that the archers rushed upon him, from whom he received a grievous wound, and flatly rejects the alternative that Saul merely feared the archers greatly, calling it by no means likely.

The grammatical-historical voices press the opposite rendering. Keil and Delitzsch argue on lexical grounds that the verb is from a root meaning to writhe with dread, not to be pierced; the Septuagint and Vulgate, they show, only reached wounded by a free and unwarranted derivation, and the emphatic very tells against it. Charles Ellicott agrees that the more accurate translation is, He was sore afraid, following Gesenius, Keil and Lange, and reads the moment psychologically: the great warrior who had towered above the fight, marked by the crown on his head, now finds skilful bowmen shooting him down from a distance where his strong arm could not reach them. His army routed, his sons dead, his captains gone, the great undaunted courage at last failed him.

Whichever word stands, the witnesses agree on the pathos of the picture. Ellicott hears, ringing in Saul's ears, the sentence spoken only hours before at En-dor by the dead prophet — To-morrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me — so that the arrows seem to carry out a doom already pronounced. John Gill notes simply that Saul was the butt of the Philistines; they aimed at his person and life, the whole weight of the battle bearing down on the one man. The commentators thus turn a contested verb into a portrait of a king gradually weakened and abandoned, driven step by step — as Ellicott says the very gradualness was meant to make his misery complete, that he might feel himself die — toward the desperate turn he makes to his armour-bearer in the next verse.
4:
Saul's last command — Draw thy sword, and thrust me through — draws from the witnesses their fullest reflection, and two questions dominate. The first is who the armour-bearer was. John Gill, Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott all report the settled Jewish tradition that he was Doeg the Edomite, promoted to that office as reward for butchering the priests at Nob (1 Sam. 22:18). Clarke, following Delaney, presses a grammatical point into terrible poetry: the text says Saul took the sword — not his own, or it would have said so, but the sword just named, the armour-bearer's. So Saul and his executioner both fell by that weapon with which they had before massacred the priests of God, as Brutus and Cassius died by the swords that stabbed Caesar. The word of David over Doeg, that God would destroy him for ever (Ps. 52:5), is thus quietly fulfilled.

The second question is what Saul's dying speech reveals. Ellicott, citing Wordsworth, hears in his censure of the uncircumcised Philistines something of that religious formalism which marked his character after his fall from God, a striking sign of spiritual blindness; Saul dreads their abuse as they had abused Samson, and clings to the sacredness of his person as the Lord's anointed even at the last. His armour-bearer would not — from love, Ellicott judges, or dread of the guilt the Amalekite would soon incur for the very deed. For 2 Sam. 1 has an Amalekite claim to have slain the leaning king. Most commentators — Keil, Lange, Bishop Hervey — take that story, as Ellicott reports, for an invention framed to extract a rich gift from David; Ewald alone credits it, and Ellicott shows the two may be harmonized: Saul, mortally wounded, fell on his sword, his armour-bearer thought him dead and followed suit, but the king rallied for a moment in which the plundering Amalekite finished the work.

Beneath the detail lies the gravest matter — the judgment on Saul himself, and the witnesses are frankly divided. Theologians are divided, Ellicott admits: S. Bernard thinks that Saul was lost for ever, and Cornelius a Lapide with Bishop Wordsworth has no kindly thought for him; but Josephus and many Rabbis write in warm and glowing terms of the patriotic devotion with which Saul went to meet his end. Without justifying the fatal act, Ellicott pleads the awful position of the king that evening and commends the fearless human sympathy of the Biblical writers, who let David's tender lament close the record and leave the first anointed of the Lord in the hands of his God. That last phrase, more than any verdict, is where these witnesses come to rest.
5:
The armour-bearer's own death is told in a single line, but the witnesses draw real weight from it. John Gill fixes the plain sense against the rival tradition: the servant saw that Saul was dead by his own hands, and not by the hands of the Amalekite — for, Gill reasons, the loyal armour-bearer would scarcely have suffered a stranger to touch his master. This is the reading that lets the double account of 2 Sam. 1 hang together, since the man could believe the king already dead the instant he fell upon his sword, even if Saul lingered long enough for the Amalekite's later, opportunist stroke. And if the sword was indeed the armour-bearer's — Doeg's own — then master and servant perish by the single blade that had once been drawn against the priests of the Lord, the instrument of Saul's worst cruelty turned at last upon his own life and his servant's.

Matthew Henry presses the moral. It was an aggravating circumstance of the death of Saul that by the example of his wickedness in murdering himself, he drew in his servant to be guilty of the same wickedness, and perished not alone in his iniquity. The contagion of a leader's sin is the lesson: what a man does in his despair does not die with him but reaches out to pull others after him. Henry had already commended the servant's earlier refusal to strike the king — no man's servant ought to be a slave to his master's lusts or passions of any kind — which makes the final surrender the more tragic, obedience curdled into imitation.

Yet even here the witnesses refuse to read the manner of death as the measure of the soul. The armour-bearer dies as Saul dies, and the same caution Henry urges over the king applies to the servant: we cannot judge of the spiritual or eternal state of any by the manner of their death. The verse stands, in their hands, as a small dark parable — devotion that follows its master even into sin, and a reminder that the strongest loyalty must still answer to God before it answers to a man. What began as a faithful refusal ends as a fatal compliance, and the witnesses let the quiet horror of that reversal speak for itself.
6:
So Saul died, and his three sons, and his armour-bearer, and all his men, that same day together. The verse gathers the whole house into one grave, and the witnesses first mark a distinction within it. Matthew Henry observes that the deaths were not alike in kind: the three sons died honourably in the field of battle, while Saul and his armour-bearer destroyed themselves — the same day, but not the same death.

The grammatical-historical readers guard the phrase all his men against a wooden literalism. Adam Clarke takes it to mean those of his troops which were his life or body guards, since the bulk of the army had fled at the first onset. Charles Ellicott agrees, correcting the verse by its parallel: 1 Chron. 10:6 explains it as all his house, and we know Ishbosheth his son and Abner his captain were not among the slain. The meaning is that Saul's household and personal staff fell fighting round him — and Ellicott cannot resist Aytoun's ballad of the Scottish king at Flodden, Knight and noble lie around him, cold, on Flodden's fatal hill, not a single man departed from his monarch, as the very image of the guard dying about King Saul on Gilboa.

John Gill pauses on the length of the reign now ended, gathering the reckonings — Josephus giving Saul eighteen years in Samuel's lifetime and twenty-two after, to make the forty years the apostle assigns him (Acts 13:21), while the heathen writer Eupolemus allows twenty-one — and concedes that chronologers are not agreed. The precise sum matters less than the shape: a reign begun in the people's demand for a king like the nations ends with king, heir, and household swept away in a single day. For all their differences of detail the witnesses read the verse as the completion of a judgment long foretold: the house of Saul falls entire, not by accident of war but as the last stroke of the sentence pronounced over his defiance — and yet, as Henry keeps insisting, the different manner of the sons' and the father's dying warns us not to read eternal destinies off a battlefield, where one event comes alike to the faithful and the fallen.
7:
The rout does not end on the hill; it empties whole regions. When the men of Israel on the other side of the valley, and on the other side Jordan, see the army fled and the king and his sons dead, they abandon their cities, and the Philistines move in. Adam Clarke reads it as sheer panic — the people panic-struck, fleeing as far as they could out of the reach of the Philistines, the dwellers beyond Jordan fearing for their safety once Beth-shan fell.

Charles Ellicott maps the collapse precisely and then limits it. The other side of the valley he takes for the country opposite the Jezreel battlefield, the land of Issachar, Zebulun and Naphtali, and the other side of Jordan for the usual phrase for the east bank; the alarm drove the villagers of the smaller towns to abandon their homes rather than face the invaders. But Ellicott is careful that the disaster was not total: the Philistines could not have penetrated very far, for shortly after Gilboa we find Abner rallying the house of Saul round Ishbosheth at Mahanaim, some twenty miles east of the river, and the country south of Jezreel escaped overrunning altogether — the presence of David in that part, he says, no doubt insured its immunity from invasion.

Matthew Henry reads the ruin theologically. He sees to what a sad pass Saul by his wickedness had brought his country, which might have remained in the hands of the uncircumcised if David had not been raised up to repair the breaches of it. And he hears in it the fulfilment of prophecy: the people, who with their king had still done wickedly, were consumed as Samuel had warned they and their king would be (1 Sam. 12:25), and long after Hosea would name the whole tragedy — I gave thee a king in mine anger, and took him away in my wrath (Hos. 13:11). See what a king he proved, Henry concludes, for whom they rejected God and Samuel. The abandoned cities are thus, for the witnesses, the visible cost of a nation's choice — the empty houses of a people who wanted a king like the nations and got one whose fall left them exposed to their oldest enemy, until David should come to win the land back.
8:
On the morrow the Philistines come to strip the slain and find the royal dead, and the witnesses read the discovery as both a providence and a warning. Matthew Henry notes the bitter irony of the timing: the pursuers, in fear of whom he slew himself, came not to the place where he was till the next day. Saul might have escaped, or at least survived the night; his self-slaughter was needless as well as sinful. But whom God will destroy he infatuates and utterly consumes with his terrors — the panic that drove the sword was itself part of the judgment.

Henry dwells on the peculiar shame reserved for Saul's body. Distinguished on the bloody field by its very length — the head-and-shoulders height he had once been proud of — the corpse becomes the object of a barbarous and brutish revenge, the Philistines insulting in death the king they had feared in life. And Henry draws the moral edge: He that thinks to save his honour by sin will certainly lose it. Saul slew himself to avoid abuse, and never was royal corpse so abused as his was — perhaps the more, if they learned he had died by his own hand.

The grammatical-historical witnesses supply the setting. Adam Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch explain the delay simply — the battle and pursuit ran on till night, leaving no time to plunder till daylight — and Keil traces the trophies' route round the idol-temples and towns of Philistia. Charles Ellicott stresses that only on the morrow did the enemy grasp the completeness of their success, so desperate had been the defence, and that the crown and bracelet were already gone, carried off in the night by the Amalekite. He tempers the horror with a note of proportion: the savagery was but a reprisal, for the same generation had seen Israel treat Goliath's severed head no better; and he lets Dante voice the curse called down on the barren hill where the Lord's anointed fell (2 Sam. 1:21). The witnesses thus hold two truths together — the real cruelty of the Philistines, and the deeper truth that the abuse Saul so dreaded overtook him precisely because he had sought by sin to escape it.
9:
The mutilation is deliberate and public. The Philistines cut off Saul's head, strip his armour, and send both round their land to publish it in the house of their idols, and among the people. Matthew Henry reads every act as theology in reverse. In cutting off the head of the tallest man in Israel — taller by the head than other men, but now shorter by the head — they meant a reproach to Israel, who promised themselves that a crowned and an anointed head would save them, and a particular reproach to Saul. Had they wished to avenge Goliath's beheading, Henry observes, they would have sought David's head, not Saul's; the insult was aimed at the crown itself.

Above all the witnesses fasten on the proclaiming in the house of their idols. Here, Henry says, the Philistines ascribe the honour of their victory, not to the real justice of the true God, but to the imaginary power of their false gods, and by that misdirected worship shame those who give not the praise of their achievements to the living God. John Wesley puts the same rebuke as a question: they cut off the head and set it, as the Chronicler adds, in the temple of Dagon (1 Chron. 10:10) — and by this respect shewn to their pretended deities, how do they shame those who give not the honour of their achievements to the living God?

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the detail. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott note that though only Saul's head and armour are named here, the parallel in the next verses shows the heads of the sons were taken too, the trophies dispersed round about the different towns and villages of Philistia and the headless bodies fastened to the wall of Beth-shan. John Gill adds that the head was fixed in Dagon's temple, perhaps the one at Ashdod. What the commentators will not let pass is the pattern: a heathen people crediting their idols for a victory the God of Israel had in fact given as judgment — David's own grief that it would be told in Gath and Ashkelon (2 Sam. 1:20) sounding already behind the triumph.
10:
The armour goes into the house of Ashtaroth and the body onto the wall of Beth-shan, and the witnesses read both as calculated dishonour. John Gill sets the custom in its ancient frame: nothing was more common among the heathen than to place in their temples the arms they took from their enemies, a practice he finds in Homer and Virgil — and, he adds, even in Israel, where Goliath's sword was laid up in the tabernacle. The armour of the Lord's anointed hung as a trophy in a pagan shrine is thus the exact inversion of that dedication.

Matthew Henry presses the sting of the goddess's name. Ashtaroth, the idol that Israel had many a time gone a whoring after, now triumphs over them — the very worship for which the nation had provoked God now seeming to crow over the nation's king. Henry notes too that they fastened the body and the bodies of his sons to the wall of Beth-shan, hung up to be devoured by the birds of prey, and reminds the reader how bitterly David would lament that the tidings should reach the Philistine cities.

The grammatical-historical commentators locate and explain the scene. Charles Ellicott weighs whether the Ashtaroth means the armour was divided among the shrines of Astarte or placed together in her famous temple at Ashkelon — which Herodotus called the most ancient of all — and thinks the latter likelier, since Ashkelon is named in David's dirge (2 Sam. 1:20). He fixes Beth-shan in Manasseh, four miles west of Jordan, and suggests its lingering Canaanite population, permitted to remain (Judg. 1:27), was why the town was chosen for so barbarous an exhibition — its old inhabitants would relish the degrading of their ancient enemies. Adam Clarke adds the practical note that the bodies were fastened, probably by means of iron hooks, in the main street or gate. Across these details the witnesses hold one thread: the studied attempt to make Saul's ruin a monument to Philistine gods — an attempt, Henry reminds us, that God would shortly overturn when David subdued the very people who now boasted.
11:
With the bodies hung in shame, the scene turns to Jabesh-Gilead, and the witnesses read the men of that city as a single, shining act of gratitude. Adam Clarke names the debt at once: this was an act of gratitude due to Saul, who, at the very commencement of his reign, rescued them from Nahash, king of the Ammonites (1 Sam. 11), and saved them from the deepest degradation. Their daring rescue and seven days' fast showed that they retained a due sense of their obligation to this unfortunate monarch.

Charles Ellicott draws out the long arc of the memory. The splendid feat of arms of their young king, done in the early fervour of his consecration, was still fresh in the city after all the years between, and now they burned to rescue the body of their hero from shame. Ellicott marvels at the fruitfulness of that first deed — it was singular, he writes, how that first deed of splendid patriotism bore fruit after so many long years — and lets Aytoun's lines carry the thought: Good deeds immortal are, they cannot die; they live, and bud and bloom, and men partake still of their freshness. He fixes the geography too: Jabesh-Gilead in Manasseh, beyond Jordan on the road from Pella to Gerasa, perhaps fourteen miles from Beth-shan, its name surviving in the Wady Yabis.

John Gill supplies the same distance in round terms — about eight miles from Beth-shan across the river — and the witnesses agree in reading the whole episode as gratitude answering an old kindness in its extremity. The lesson they draw is unforced: a benefit conferred long ago, when Saul was at his best, is repaid at the very hour when he is most helpless and most abused, and by men who owed him everything and could now gain nothing. Nor were they close at hand; the distance and the peril of the night crossing only sharpen the debt they felt bound to pay. In a chapter of judgment and shame, the loyalty of Jabesh stands as the one bright thread — proof, as Ellicott says, that a generous deed outlives the doer and returns to honour him when all else has failed.
12:
The valiant men of Jabesh cross by night, take down the bodies from the wall of Beth-shan, carry them home, and burn them — and it is that burning that divides the witnesses. The difficulty is real: cremation was not the way of Israel. Charles Ellicott states the rule plainly — burning a corpse was never the custom in Israel, and was restricted to criminals convicted of a crime of the deepest dye (Lev. 20:14); the Jews in all cases buried their dead. On that ground the Chaldee, and many after it, read the act as the solemn burning of spices, the honour later paid at the graves of the kings of Judah, as at Asa's great burning (2 Chron. 16:14).

Adam Clarke resists the softening and argues for a literal burning of the bodies — but from necessity, not disgrace. They could not have buried them about Beth-shan without being discovered, and embalming was most likely out of all question, as doubtless the bodies were now too putrid to bear it. So they burnt them because there was no other way of disposing of them at that time so as to do them honour, preserving the bones and ashes for burial. Ellicott finally meets Clarke halfway: since the text says expressly they burnt them, and the exposed trunks had lain for days and were no doubt in a state of putrefaction, he concludes the flesh here only was burned, while the bones were reverently and lovingly preserved.

Matthew Henry keeps the emphasis on honour however the word be taken — they made a burning for the bodies, to perfume them, as was done for Asa, or else burnt the putrefying flesh, but in either case to do their dead king reverence. John Wesley returns to the motive: this they did, not only out of a concern for the honour of Israel, and the crown of Israel, but out of gratitude to Saul, for his zeal and forwardness to rescue them from the Ammonites. Beneath the philological dispute the witnesses are agreed on the heart of it — that whether by fire or by fragrant spices, the men of Jabesh were bent on rescuing the mangled remains of Saul and his sons from shame and rendering them the last honours a grateful people could give.
13:
The bones are laid under a tree at Jabesh and the city fasts seven days, and the witnesses gather here to close the book. John Gill lingers on the tree — an oak, he says, like the oak over Deborah's grave — and reports the old fancy that the Jews buried beneath such trees for a parable of hope: as the tree, seemingly dead in winter, has every spring an annual resurrection, so men's dry bones shall have new sap put into them at the day of judgment. Charles Ellicott names it the well-known tamarisk, recalls Saul's own love of trees, and follows the bones forward: David would afterward fetch these royal remains and lay them in the family sepulchre of Kish at Zelah (2 Sam. 21:12). The seven-day fast he links to the seven days Israel mourned Jacob (Gen. 50:10) and to the later Jewish rule of mourning. John Wesley cautions that the fasting need not be taken strictly, but as the ancients used the word — eating little and mean food, and drinking water, for that time.

Over the grave the deepest debate of the chapter comes to rest: was Saul a self-murderer? Adam Clarke answers with a flat and famous No. Saul was already mortally wounded when he fell on the sword; his act was a fit of desperation, the issue of a mind greatly agitated and full of distraction, and there is not a coroner's inquest in this nation, he writes, that would not have brought in a verdict of derangement. Clarke closes his whole commentary on Saul with a plea for mercy — that the pious and the humane would everywhere have consoled themselves with the hope that God had extended mercy to his soul. Matthew Henry is sterner on the deed, having called Saul's death that of a fool and a self-murderer, yet even he refuses the final verdict: the Scripture makes no mention what became of the souls of Saul and his sons; secret things belong not to us.

And here Henry and Wesley together sound the book's last note. This book began with the birth of Samuel, they observe, but now it ends with the burial of Saul — and the comparing of the two will teach us to prefer the honour that comes from God before any of the honours which this world pretends to have the disposal of. The tree at Jabesh, the fast, the rescued bones — all the poor honours men can pay the dead — send the witnesses back to the one honour that matters, and leave the first king, as Ellicott said, in the hands of his God.
